---
title: "A Note to the Detective"
---

This book invites you to step into the role of a detective—not of crimes in dark
alleys, but of claims made in the name of science.

In each case, you are presented with evidence: a study summary, a striking chart,
a table of results, or—get your magnifying glasses ready—a model output. The
claims may sound convincing. They are often confidently stated, widely shared,
and sometimes even well-intentioned. Your task is to examine the scene, inspect
the exhibits, and decide for yourself whether the conclusions hold up—or whether
something has gone wrong.

The cases in this book focus on different kinds of failures. Some involve causal
claims that go beyond what the data can support—thus the title of this book.
Others hinge on poor measurement, selective reporting, misuse of AI systems,
misleading visualizations, or statistical traps that are easy to fall into and
hard to spot at first glance. Not every case ends with a conviction: a few are
included precisely because the evidence is solid, the reasoning careful, and the
conclusions warranted. Learning to recognize good science is just as important as
learning to detect bad science.

There is one thing that is particularly important to us: This is not a book about
science-bashing. Quite the opposite. Science is the best tool we have for
understanding the world, and a lot of scientific work is done carefully, honestly,
and with humility. But science is also a human enterprise. It involves imperfect
data, simplifying assumptions, incentives to communicate dramatically,
and—especially outside academic journals—shortcuts that can blur important
nuances. When results travel from papers to press releases to headlines, subtle
problems can easily turn into bold but misleading claims. And yes, sometimes the
data, analyses, or conclusions are flawed in the first place.

The detective analogy is useful because solving these cases rarely requires
advanced technical knowledge. What it does require is careful reading, logical
consistency, attention to what is—and is not—being shown, and a healthy dose of
common sense. Like a good investigator, you will learn to ask simple questions:
What is missing from the data? What would have to be true for this claim to hold?
Could this pattern arise for a more mundane reason?

If this book does its job, you will finish it with sharper instincts: a better
sense for when to be impressed, when to be skeptical, and when to withhold
judgment. The goal is not to turn you into a cynic, but into a careful reader of
evidence—someone who appreciates the power of science while being alert to the
ways it can be misused, misunderstood, or oversold.

Welcome to the crime scenes.
